news_summary: Recent advancements showcase breakthroughs in manipulating 2D crystals with light and magnetism, responsible AI development, and commercial progress in quantum entanglement technology.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes témoignent de percées dans la manipulation des cristaux 2D à l'aide de la lumière et du magnétisme, dans le développement d'une IA responsable et dans les progrès commerciaux de la technologie de l'intrication quantique.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances muestran avances revolucionarios en la manipulación de cristales 2D con luz y magnetismo, el desarrollo responsable de la IA y los progresos comerciales en la tecnología del entrelazamiento cuántico.

articles.title: A tiny twist creates giant magnetic skyrmions in 2D crystals
articles.summary: Twisting atomically thin magnetic layers does more than reshape their electronics—it can create giant, topological magnetic textures. In chromium triiodide, researchers observed skyrmion-like patterns stretching far beyond the expected moiré scale, reaching hundreds of nanometers. Even more surprising, their size doesn’t simply follow the twist pattern but peaks at a specific angle. This twist-controlled magnetism could pave the way for low-power spintronic devices built from geometry alone.
articles.content:
In the fast moving field of two dimensional materials, even a slight rotational shift between layers can dramatically change how a material behaves. Scientists previously discovered that when atom thin crystals are stacked with a small angular mismatch, their electronic properties can transform. This approach, known as moiré engineering, has become a key strategy for designing new forms of quantum matter.

Now researchers report in Nature Nanotechnology that magnetism can also behave in surprising ways under these conditions. In twisted antiferromagnetic layers, magnetic spin patterns are not limited to the small repeating moiré unit cell. Instead, they can spread into much larger, topological structures that extend across hundreds of nanometers.

Giant Magnetic Textures Beyond the Moiré Pattern

In most moiré systems, the size of physical effects is determined directly by the interference pattern created when two crystal lattices overlap. Magnetic order in stacked van der Waals magnets was widely expected to follow this same length scale. The new findings challenge that assumption.

The team examined twisted double bilayer chromium triiodide (CrI 3 ) using scanning nitrogen-vacancy magnetometry, a technique that images magnetic fields with nanoscale precision. They observed magnetic textures reaching distances of up to ~300 nm, far exceeding the size of a single moiré cell and roughly ten times larger than the underlying wavelength.

A Counterintuitive Twist Angle Effect

The results reveal an unexpected pattern. When the twist angle becomes smaller, the moiré wavelength increases. However, the magnetic textures do not simply grow along with it. Instead, their size changes in the opposite way, reaching a maximum near 1.1° and disappearing above ~2°.

This reversal shows that magnetism is not just copying the moiré template. Rather, it arises from a balance between several competing forces, including exchange interactions, magnetic anisotropy and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions. All of these are subtly adjusted by how the layers are rotated relative to one another. Large scale spin dynamics simulations back up this interpretation, demonstrating the formation of extended Néel-type antiferromagnetic skyrmions that span multiple moiré cells.

Skyrmions and Low Power Spintronics

These findings matter beyond basic physics. Skyrmions are promising for future information technologies because they are small, stable and protected by their topology. They can also be moved using very little energy. Creating them simply by adjusting the twist angle, without lithography, heavy metals or strong electric currents, provides a clean and geometry driven path toward low power spintronic devices.

The researchers describe this phenomenon as super-moiré spin order, highlighting that twist engineering operates across multiple scales. A change in atomic alignment can generate topological structures on much larger, mesoscale distances. This challenges the long held idea that moiré physics is only a local effect and positions twist angle as a powerful thermodynamic control parameter capable of tuning exchange, anisotropy and chiral interactions to stabilize topological phases.

From a practical standpoint, these large and robust Néel-type skyrmionic textures are well suited for integration into devices. Their larger size makes them easier to detect and manipulate. At the same time, their topological protection and insulating host material suggest extremely low energy loss during operation. As scientists continue to explore how geometry shapes quantum behavior, such emergent magnetic states could play an important role in developing energy efficient, post-CMOS computing technologies.

Dr. Elton Santos, Reader in Theoretical/Computational Condensed Matter Physics, University of Edinburgh, whose team led the modelling aspect of the project, said: "This discovery shows that twisting is not just an electronic knob, but a magnetic one. We're seeing collective spin order self-organize on scales far larger than the moiré lattice. It opens the door to designing topological magnetic states simply by controlling angle, which is a remarkably simple handle with profound practical consequences."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in controlling magnetic properties in 2D materials via twisting layers, enabling creation of large, stable skyrmions that can lead to low-power spintronic devices. This has broad implications for future energy-efficient computing technologies, representing a meaningful advance in materials science and technology with potential large-scale societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: Twisting 2D crystals creates giant magnetic patterns for future devices
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study materials made of layers only a few atoms thick, called two-dimensional (2D) materials. When these layers are stacked with a small twist between them, their properties can change in surprising ways. This twisting method, known as moiré engineering, has mainly affected electronic behavior until now.

**What happened** – Researchers working with chromium triiodide (CrI3), a 2D magnetic material, found that twisting two bilayers at certain angles produces very large magnetic patterns called skyrmions. These skyrmions are magnetic whirlpools that stretch much farther than expected—up to 300 nanometers, about ten times bigger than the usual repeating pattern formed by twisting. The largest skyrmions appeared near a twist of 1.1°, and they disappeared when the twist was above 2°.

**Impact** – This discovery shows magnetism can be controlled by twisting, not just electronics. The skyrmions created this way are bigger and easier to detect, and they form without needing complicated tools or strong electric currents. Because skyrmions can be moved with very little energy, this method could help build new, low-power devices that use the spin of electrons (spintronics) for computing and memory.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore how to use this twist control to design practical devices that use these large magnetic skyrmions. They will also study other materials and twist angles to better understand and improve this effect. This research opens a path for making energy-efficient technologies based on geometry and magnetism.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Twisting ultrathin magnetic layers creates large, stable skyrmions that could lead to new low-energy computing devices controlled simply by adjusting the twist angle.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un léger twist crée de grands motifs magnétiques dans les cristaux 2D
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des matériaux composés de couches seulement quelques atomes d’épaisseur, appelés matériaux bidimensionnels (2D). Lorsque ces couches sont empilées avec un léger angle de rotation entre elles, leurs propriétés peuvent changer de façon surprenante. Cette méthode de torsion, appelée ingénierie moiré, affectait surtout le comportement électronique jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs travaillant sur le triiodure de chrome (CrI3), un matériau magnétique 2D, ont découvert qu’en tordant deux doubles couches à certains angles, on crée de très grands motifs magnétiques appelés skyrmions. Ces skyrmions sont des tourbillons magnétiques qui s’étendent beaucoup plus loin que prévu – jusqu’à 300 nanomètres, soit dix fois plus grand que le motif répétitif habituel formé par la torsion. Les plus grands skyrmions apparaissent autour de 1,1° de torsion et disparaissent au-delà de 2°.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le magnétisme peut être contrôlé par la torsion, pas seulement l’électronique. Les skyrmions créés ainsi sont plus grands et plus faciles à détecter, et ils se forment sans outils compliqués ni forts courants électriques. Comme les skyrmions peuvent se déplacer avec très peu d’énergie, cette méthode pourrait aider à construire de nouveaux dispositifs à faible consommation utilisant le spin des électrons (spintronique) pour l’informatique et la mémoire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont explorer comment utiliser ce contrôle par torsion pour concevoir des dispositifs pratiques utilisant ces grands skyrmions magnétiques. Ils étudieront aussi d’autres matériaux et angles de torsion pour mieux comprendre et améliorer cet effet. Cette recherche ouvre une voie pour créer des technologies écoénergétiques basées sur la géométrie et le magnétisme.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La torsion de couches magnétiques ultrafines crée de grands skyrmions stables qui pourraient mener à de nouveaux dispositifs informatiques à faible consommation, contrôlés simplement par l’angle de torsion.
articles.personality_title_es: Un pequeño giro crea grandes patrones magnéticos en cristales 2D
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian materiales formados por capas de solo unos pocos átomos de grosor, llamados materiales bidimensionales (2D). Cuando estas capas se apilan con un pequeño giro entre ellas, sus propiedades pueden cambiar de forma sorprendente. Este método de giro, conocido como ingeniería moiré, hasta ahora afectaba principalmente el comportamiento electrónico.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores que trabajan con triyoduro de cromo (CrI3), un material magnético 2D, descubrieron que al girar dos doble capas a ciertos ángulos se crean patrones magnéticos muy grandes llamados skyrmions. Estos skyrmions son remolinos magnéticos que se extienden mucho más allá de lo esperado, hasta 300 nanómetros, unas diez veces más grandes que el patrón repetitivo normal formado por el giro. Los skyrmions más grandes aparecieron cerca de un giro de 1,1° y desaparecieron cuando el giro superó los 2°.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que el magnetismo puede controlarse con el giro, no solo la electrónica. Los skyrmions creados de esta forma son más grandes y fáciles de detectar, y se forman sin herramientas complicadas ni fuertes corrientes eléctricas. Como los skyrmions se pueden mover con muy poca energía, este método podría ayudar a construir nuevos dispositivos de baja energía que usen el spin de los electrones (spintrónica) para computación y memoria.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán cómo usar este control por giro para diseñar dispositivos prácticos que usen estos grandes skyrmions magnéticos. También estudiarán otros materiales y ángulos de giro para entender y mejorar este efecto. Esta investigación abre el camino para crear tecnologías eficientes en energía basadas en la geometría y el magnetismo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Girar capas magnéticas ultrafinas crea skyrmions grandes y estables que podrían llevar a nuevos dispositivos computacionales de baja energía controlados simplemente por el ángulo de giro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two overlapping, semi-transparent hexagonal crystal lattices gently twisted at a slight angle, with glowing, swirling magnetic spin patterns forming large, intricate circular skyrmion textures that extend well beyond the small moiré cells, all rendered in soft natural hues of amber, deep green, and muted gold to evoke a sense of subtle quantum harmony and layered complexity.

articles.title: Scientists just turned light into a remote control for crystals
articles.summary: NYU researchers have found a way to use light to control how microscopic particles assemble into crystals, effectively turning illumination into a tool for shaping matter. By adding light-sensitive molecules to a liquid filled with tiny particles, they can adjust how strongly the particles attract or repel one another simply by changing the light’s intensity or pattern. This allows them to trigger crystals to form, dissolve, or even be reshaped in real time.
articles.content:
Scientists at NYU have developed a way to use light to guide how microscopic particles arrange themselves into crystals. The work, reported in the Cell Press journal Chem, describes a straightforward and reversible technique for building crystals that could support the creation of a new class of responsive, adaptable materials.

Crystals appear everywhere in nature and technology, from snowflakes and diamonds to the silicon inside electronic devices. At their core, crystals consist of particles organized in precise, repeating patterns. To better understand how these structures emerge, researchers often study colloidal particles, which are tiny spheres suspended in liquid that naturally assemble into ordered arrangements known as colloidal crystals. These particles also serve as key components in advanced materials used in optical and photonic applications such as sensors and lasers.

Although crystals are common and highly useful, controlling exactly how and when they form has remained a major obstacle.

"The challenge in the field has been control: crystals usually form where and when they want, and once conditions are set, you have limited ability to adjust the process in real time," said study author Stefano Sacanna, professor of chemistry at NYU.

Using Photoacids to Control Particle Interactions

In their Chem study, the team identified a surprisingly simple method for directing crystal formation: shining light on the system.

The researchers introduced light sensitive molecules known as photoacids into a liquid containing colloidal particles. When exposed to light, these photoacids briefly become more acidic. That change affects how they interact with the surfaces of the particles, altering the particles' electric charge. By modifying the charge, the scientists can control whether the particles pull together and stick or push apart and separate.

"Essentially, we used light as a remote control to program how matter organizes itself at the microscale," said Sacanna.

Real Time Crystal Growth and Melting

Through a combination of experiments and computer simulations, the researchers demonstrated that adjusting the brightness, duration, and pattern of light allows them to direct crystal behavior with remarkable precision. They can initiate crystal growth or cause crystals to dissolve whenever they choose. They can determine where crystallization occurs, reshape and "sculpt" the structures, and enhance their uniformity and size to create larger and more intricate colloidal assemblies.

"Using our photoacid gave us a surprising level of control over the attraction between particles. Just turning the light up or down a little made the difference between the particle fully sticking or being fully free," said study author Steven van Kesteren of ETH Zürich, who conducted this work at NYU as a postdoctoral researcher in Sacanna's lab.

"Because light is so easy to control, we could make our system do quite complex things. We could shoot light at particle blobs and see them melt under the microscope, or shine a light so that random blobs of particles ordered themselves into crystals. We could also remove specific crystals quite easily by simply unsticking the particles at that spot," added van Kesteren.

One Pot Setup With Reversible Assembly

A notable advantage of this approach is that it works as a "one pot" experiment. The team did not need to redesign particles or repeatedly adjust salt concentrations in separate trials. By simply changing the level of illumination, they could prompt particles to assemble into crystals or break apart again.

Toward Light Programmable Materials

This advance points toward materials whose internal structure, and therefore their properties, can be tuned using light. For example, photonic materials could have their color or optical response written, erased, and rewritten on demand. Light programmable colloidal crystals may eventually enable reconfigurable optical coatings, adaptive sensors, and next generation display and data storage technologies, where patterns and functions are defined dynamically by illumination rather than fixed during manufacturing.

"Our approach brings us closer to dynamic, programmable colloidal materials that can be reconfigured on demand," said study author Glen Hocky, associate professor of chemistry and a faculty member at the Simons Center for Computational Physical Chemistry at NYU. "This system also allows us to test a number of predictions on how self-assembly should behave when interactions between particles or molecules are changing across space or time."

Additional study authors include Nicole Smina, Shihao Zang, and Cheuk Wai Leung of NYU. The research was supported by the US Army Research Office (award W911NF-21-1-0011), the Swiss National Science Foundation (grant 217966), and the NYU Simons Center for Computational Physical Chemistry (grant 839534).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers developed a novel, reversible method to control crystal formation using light. This advancement has broad implications for creating dynamic, programmable materials with potential applications in optics, sensors, and data storage, benefiting society by enabling new technologies. The article provides detailed context and explains the method and its potential impact clearly.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use light to control crystal formation in real time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Crystals are made of tiny particles arranged in neat patterns and are found in many natural and technological materials. However, controlling how and when these crystals form has been difficult for scientists.

**What happened** – Researchers at New York University (NYU) discovered a way to use light to guide how microscopic particles come together to form crystals. They added special molecules called photoacids to a liquid with tiny particles. When light shines on the liquid, these photoacids change the electric charge of the particles, making them attract or repel each other. By changing the light’s brightness and pattern, the scientists could start, stop, or reshape crystals whenever they wanted.

**Impact** – This method is simple and reversible, meaning crystals can be built and broken apart repeatedly just by changing the light. It works without needing to change the particles or the liquid’s makeup. This gives researchers precise control over crystal growth, which could lead to new materials whose properties, like color or how they handle light, can be changed instantly with light. Such materials might be used in better sensors, displays, or data storage devices.

**What's next step** – The team plans to explore how this light-controlled system can be used to create materials that can be programmed and reprogrammed for different uses. They also want to test how changing interactions between particles affects the way crystals form and behave over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NYU scientists invented a way to use light as a remote control to build and reshape crystals instantly, opening doors to new, adaptable materials.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques utilisent la lumière pour contrôler la formation des cristaux en temps réel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cristaux sont composés de petites particules organisées en motifs réguliers et se trouvent dans de nombreux matériaux naturels et technologiques. Cependant, il a été difficile pour les scientifiques de contrôler comment et quand ces cristaux se forment.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de New York (NYU) ont découvert une méthode pour utiliser la lumière afin de guider l’assemblage de particules microscopiques en cristaux. Ils ont ajouté des molécules spéciales appelées photoacides dans un liquide contenant de minuscules particules. Lorsque la lumière éclaire ce liquide, ces photoacides modifient la charge électrique des particules, les faisant s’attirer ou se repousser. En changeant l’intensité et le motif de la lumière, les scientifiques peuvent démarrer, arrêter ou remodeler les cristaux quand ils le désirent.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est simple et réversible, ce qui signifie que les cristaux peuvent être construits et détruits plusieurs fois simplement en modifiant la lumière. Elle fonctionne sans avoir besoin de changer les particules ou la composition du liquide. Cela donne un contrôle précis sur la croissance des cristaux, ce qui pourrait permettre de créer de nouveaux matériaux dont les propriétés, comme la couleur ou la gestion de la lumière, peuvent être modifiées instantanément par la lumière. Ces matériaux pourraient être utilisés pour fabriquer de meilleurs capteurs, écrans ou dispositifs de stockage de données.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe souhaite explorer comment ce système contrôlé par la lumière peut servir à créer des matériaux programmables et reprogrammables pour différentes utilisations. Ils veulent aussi étudier comment les interactions entre particules influencent la formation et le comportement des cristaux dans le temps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques de NYU ont inventé un moyen d’utiliser la lumière comme télécommande pour construire et remodeler instantanément des cristaux, ouvrant la voie à des matériaux nouveaux et adaptables.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan luz para controlar la formación de cristales en tiempo real
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los cristales están hechos de pequeñas partículas organizadas en patrones ordenados y se encuentran en muchos materiales naturales y tecnológicos. Sin embargo, controlar cómo y cuándo se forman estos cristales ha sido difícil para los científicos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Nueva York (NYU) descubrieron una forma de usar la luz para guiar cómo las partículas microscópicas se unen para formar cristales. Añadieron moléculas especiales llamadas fotoácidos a un líquido con partículas diminutas. Cuando la luz ilumina el líquido, estos fotoácidos cambian la carga eléctrica de las partículas, haciendo que se atraigan o se repelan. Al cambiar el brillo y el patrón de la luz, los científicos pueden iniciar, detener o remodelar los cristales cuando quieran.

**Impacto** – Este método es simple y reversible, lo que significa que los cristales pueden formarse y deshacerse repetidamente solo cambiando la luz. Funciona sin necesidad de modificar las partículas o el líquido. Esto da un control preciso sobre el crecimiento de cristales, lo que podría llevar a nuevos materiales cuyas propiedades, como el color o la forma en que manejan la luz, puedan cambiarse al instante con la luz. Tales materiales podrían usarse en mejores sensores, pantallas o dispositivos de almacenamiento de datos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea explorar cómo este sistema controlado por luz puede usarse para crear materiales programables y reprogramables para diferentes usos. También quieren estudiar cómo cambian las interacciones entre partículas y cómo eso afecta la formación y comportamiento de los cristales con el tiempo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos de NYU inventaron una manera de usar la luz como control remoto para construir y remodelar cristales al instante, abriendo la puerta a materiales nuevos y adaptables.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of glowing microscopic spheres gently assembling into intricate crystal patterns under the soft illumination of a radiant, abstract light source, with subtle waves of color representing the dynamic control of particle interactions in a fluid environment.

articles.title: AI agents are ‘aeroplanes for the mind’: five ways to ensure that scientists are responsible pilots
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00665-yAs artificial-intelligence systems take on more of the scientific workflow, the central goal should not be complete automation, but designing platforms that preserve creativity, responsibility and surprise.
articles.content:
In the early 1980s, Apple co-founder Steve Jobs described the computer as “a bicycle for our minds”. He was inspired by a Scientific American graphic he’d encountered as a boy, showing that a human on a bicycle is more energy-efficient than any animal1. The metaphor captured the promise of personal computing: tools that enable people to go further and faster with less effort. But the deeper brilliance of bicycles lies in what they do not do: they do not mimic human biology, nor any form found in nature. The bicycle reimagined motion entirely.

By comparison, I propose that artificial-intelligence agents are aeroplanes for the mind — they can speed things up for humans even more than bicycles do, but they are harder to control and the consequences of mistakes can be huge. And scientists are particularly poised to benefit from these tools. Scientific research is, at its core, a journey into the unknown. Yet working in new terrains brings unexpected challenges2 and frequent failures3.

Why we don’t really know what the public thinks about science

To push the frontiers of knowledge forwards quickly and responsibly, science and scientists urgently need a playbook for flying these aeroplanes. In my view, effective use of AI in research will probably require the development of AI agents that are grounded in robust, domain-specific scientific information. The real question is not whether machines will replace scientists, but what kind of scientists we will become when we learn to fly them.

To put this into practice, my team developed SciSciGPT4, a prototype multi-agent system in which several specialized AI agents divide and coordinate research workflows. We used the science of science5 — a field that combines large data sets and computational methods to probe the dynamics of scientific progress.

At the heart of SciSciGPT is the ResearchManager agent. It orchestrates the workflow, dividing a researcher’s natural-language query entered through a chat interface into tasks then delegating them to agents that specialize in literature review, data extraction or analysis. These agents plan and execute sub-tasks — retrieving publications, writing code, running analyses and generating figures — while the EvaluationSpecialist continuously audits their output. Each step is logged, creating a transparent end-to-end provenance record.

In our case studies4, SciSciGPT created a visualization of collaborations between a group of universities and tested whether a figure in a paper could be replicated from data in its repository. It completed these research tasks faster and with higher-quality results than experienced researchers did using AI tools.

Here, I outline the lessons that my team learnt from building a research-focused AI agent and the principles that scientists should consider when using agents for science.

Collaboration beats automation

The temptation today is to fully automate scientific workflows6–8, switching to ‘AI scientists’ or ‘self-driving laboratories’ that generate hypotheses, design experiments and draft manuscripts end-to-end. These systems can be dazzling, but science is not an assembly line, nor does it have fixed objectives to optimize. It is an enterprise that is built on interpretation, contestation and responsibility, in which human judgement is crucial.

For example, a fully automated system could conduct Newton’s prism experiments, measuring how white light splits as it passes through a prism and fitting those data to a model. But Newton did something categorically different: he reversed the set-up, recomposing the coloured beams back into white light, decisively showing that colour belongs to light itself, not to the glass. That act — deciding that an apparent anomaly was the phenomenon rather than an error to eliminate — was a leap of interpretation, not computation. Automated workflows, by design, smooth out anomalies and optimize towards fit. Scientists, by contrast, exploit surprise.

We need a new ethics for a world of AI agents

As AI tools become central to research, science faces not only a technological inflection point but also a civic one. The legitimacy of science rests on a shared social contract: that conclusions are open to scrutiny, that authors stand behind their evidence and that knowledge is produced in good faith for the public good.

In an era when public confidence in science is already fragile, this is the moment to strengthen the foundations that sustain it and to renew that contract by embedding transparency, traceability and accountability into the infrastructure of discovery itself. Full automation might deliver some answers, but it would erode the credibility that gives those answers meaning.

The more durable path is pilot-in-command science, in which the researcher — the captain — is assisted by an ensemble of agents that act as the crew and serve the best interests of the captain. The crew would be made up of an analyst agent to draft, a critic to probe, a planner to map next steps and an orchestrator to keep them in sync.

Interfaces should be built for steerability and disagreement, inviting researchers to inspect reasoning, compare alternatives and override conclusions. And human scientists should retain authority over — and responsibility for — framing the question, validating the path and signing off on conclusions. Making this model robust will require deliberate collaboration between scientists specializing in the domain of the study, engineers who work on AI, designers and ethicists to ensure that agents amplify human creativity rather than replace it.

Throughout history, discoveries have been made by humans. As AI becomes capable of contributing to discovery, the central question is not what machines can do alone, but how we design them to keep science accountable and reproducible.

Speed by itself is transformative

When the cost of failure collapses, riskier and more ambitious ideas become rational, making it practical to test questions that were once too costly or time-consuming.

Genomics illustrates this shift: decoding the first human genome took more than a decade and billions of dollars; today, sequencing costs less than US$1,000 and takes hours, transforming the field from studies of individual genes to broad exploration of entire genomes. And with the shift came fresh vantage points, enabling researchers to see connections across the scientific landscape that were otherwise invisible.

Speed also changes who can ask the questions. Lowered technical and temporal barriers enable small labs, newcomers and even individual researchers to tackle analyses that once required large teams and months of coordination. But the same forces that accelerate discovery can also amplify error. Fast science without reflection risks converging on mistakes at scale. This reinforces the importance of human–AI collaboration rather than full automation.

Agents should specialize

SciSciGPT was a natural first test case: the science of science is rich in data and methodologically diverse, and it studies how discovery itself works. But the same idea applies across disciplines, although the training data that grounds these agents will differ. Each field has its own foundations — its texts, data sets, tools and standards. In chemistry, this might mean databases tied to kinetic models that predict reaction rates and highlight where experiments tend to fail; in biomedicine, clinical guidelines linked to trial data, diagnostic protocols and multimodal patient information; in mathematics, formalized proof libraries.

AI research agents will look different in each field, but they should follow the same basic rules: results should be traceable, methods verifiable and responsibilities assigned clearly. Establishing those rules will require coordination between scientific societies, funders, journals, public research infrastructures and the AI labs building today’s models. The goal is a shared public–private framework for interoperability — for instance, common standards for logging agent decisions so that an analysis run in one lab can be audited or reproduced by another.

Some laboratories are trying to automate research work by using ‘AI scientists’ that perform projects from start to finish.Credit: Qilai Shen/Bloomberg via Getty

My team’s research shows that AI’s benefits to science are widespread across disciplines9. But when we analysed university syllabuses to examine how much each discipline teaches AI-related courses, we found a systematic mismatch: AI education is concentrated in computer science, mathematics and engineering, even though disciplines that could benefit just as much — from medicine and psychology to economics — offer much less training9.

At the same time, academia remains organized around departmental silos that drift farther apart as the burden of knowledge rises. Science policymakers should recognize these frictions and support institute-style structures that cut across disciplines and enable sustained collaboration between AI experts and domain scientists.

Trust must be engineered

When bicycles crash, the consequences are generally localized. Aeroplanes are different: when they crash, it can be catastrophic for everyone on board, often with collateral damage on the ground. That is the difference in scale that we face with AI agents. As they flourish, their failures won’t just inconvenience a single researcher; they could mislead fields, redirect funding and erode public trust in science.

One crucial advantage of large language models (LLMs) is that they can write. This means that they can document everything that they do in plain-language text. In our SciSciGPT project, every step, every line of code and every decision that the system generated was logged automatically by an LLM. The result was an overwhelming amount of data, but it was transformative. Even when my best students do an experiment, I cannot expect to see or reconstruct every step that led them to a result. With agents, I can.

AI could transform research assessment — and some academics are worried

Yet this brings another challenge: too much information. Some researchers complain that checking the AI’s output can take longer than doing the work themselves. The solution is to log not more, but better: to design systems that turn raw provenance into understanding.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant advancement in AI-assisted scientific research through the development of specialized AI agents that enhance creativity, responsibility, and transparency in science. This represents a meaningful technological breakthrough with broad implications for accelerating and improving scientific discovery across disciplines, while emphasizing ethical considerations and human oversight. The detailed explanation of the SciSciGPT system and its potential impact on research workflows shows substantial substance and positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: New AI system helps scientists work faster while keeping control and creativity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are starting to use artificial intelligence (AI) to help with research. But instead of fully automating science, experts say AI should support human creativity and careful thinking.

**What happened** – A research team created SciSciGPT, a special AI system where different AI agents work together to handle parts of scientific tasks. For example, one agent reads papers, another checks data, and another runs analyses. The system was tested on real research problems and completed them faster and more accurately than experienced researchers using AI tools.

**Impact** – This AI system is important because it doesn’t try to replace scientists but helps them work better. It keeps scientists in charge, making sure that research stays trustworthy and creative. The system also records every step clearly so people can check the work later. This balance is different from fully automated AI that might miss surprising discoveries or make mistakes.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to build more AI agents specialized for different fields like chemistry or medicine. They also want to create rules so AI work can be checked and shared easily between labs. Training scientists in AI across many subjects and encouraging teamwork between AI experts and researchers will help make these tools even better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – SciSciGPT shows how AI can speed up science while keeping human judgment and responsibility at the center of discovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau système d’IA aide les scientifiques à travailler plus vite tout en gardant le contrôle et la créativité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques commencent à utiliser l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour les aider dans leurs recherches. Mais au lieu d’automatiser complètement la science, les experts disent que l’IA doit soutenir la créativité humaine et la réflexion soigneuse.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de recherche a créé SciSciGPT, un système d’IA spécial où différents agents d’IA travaillent ensemble pour gérer des parties des tâches scientifiques. Par exemple, un agent lit des articles, un autre vérifie les données, et un autre réalise les analyses. Le système a été testé sur de vrais problèmes de recherche et les a terminés plus rapidement et plus précisément que des chercheurs expérimentés utilisant des outils d’IA.

**Impact** – Ce système d’IA est important car il ne cherche pas à remplacer les scientifiques, mais à les aider à mieux travailler. Il garde les scientifiques en contrôle, assurant que la recherche reste fiable et créative. Le système enregistre aussi chaque étape clairement pour que le travail puisse être vérifié plus tard. Cet équilibre est différent d’une IA totalement automatisée qui pourrait manquer des découvertes surprenantes ou faire des erreurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent créer plus d’agents d’IA spécialisés pour différents domaines comme la chimie ou la médecine. Ils veulent aussi établir des règles pour que le travail de l’IA puisse être vérifié et partagé facilement entre les laboratoires. Former les scientifiques à l’IA dans plusieurs disciplines et encourager la collaboration entre experts en IA et chercheurs aidera à améliorer ces outils.

**Message clé en une phrase** – SciSciGPT montre comment l’IA peut accélérer la science tout en plaçant le jugement et la responsabilité humains au cœur de la découverte.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo sistema de IA ayuda a científicos a trabajar más rápido manteniendo control y creatividad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos están empezando a usar inteligencia artificial (IA) para ayudar en la investigación. Pero en lugar de automatizar completamente la ciencia, los expertos dicen que la IA debe apoyar la creatividad humana y el pensamiento cuidadoso.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigación creó SciSciGPT, un sistema especial de IA donde diferentes agentes trabajan juntos para manejar partes de tareas científicas. Por ejemplo, un agente lee artículos, otro revisa datos y otro realiza análisis. El sistema se probó en problemas reales y los completó más rápido y con más precisión que investigadores experimentados usando herramientas de IA.

**Impacto** – Este sistema de IA es importante porque no intenta reemplazar a los científicos, sino ayudarlos a trabajar mejor. Mantiene a los científicos a cargo, asegurando que la investigación sea confiable y creativa. Además, el sistema registra cada paso claramente para que el trabajo pueda ser revisado después. Este equilibrio es diferente a la IA totalmente automatizada que podría perder descubrimientos sorprendentes o cometer errores.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren crear más agentes de IA especializados para diferentes campos como química o medicina. También quieren establecer reglas para que el trabajo de la IA pueda ser revisado y compartido fácilmente entre laboratorios. Capacitar a científicos en IA en muchas disciplinas y fomentar la colaboración entre expertos en IA y investigadores ayudará a mejorar estas herramientas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – SciSciGPT muestra cómo la IA puede acelerar la ciencia manteniendo el juicio y la responsabilidad humana en el centro del descubrimiento.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, stylized human brain gently piloting a sleek paper airplane soaring above an open book filled with glowing scientific diagrams and data streams, with smaller coordinated paper airplane agents circling around it like a supportive crew, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and blues.

articles.title: Inside the company selling quantum entanglement
articles.summary: Cables underneath New York City are teeming with entangled quantum particles of light thanks to Qunnect, a company that has spent a decade working on building an unhackable quantum internet
articles.content:
Cables underneath New York City are teeming with entangled quantum particles of light thanks to Qunnect, a company that has spent a decade working on building an unhackable quantum internet

Qunnect’s Carina rack for quantum entanglement Qunnect

Mehdi Namazi wants to sell you quantum entanglement.

He and his colleagues at Qunnect have spent nearly a decade building devices that make sharing quantum-entangled particles of light, or photons, practical enough to be used for unhackable communication.

At Qunnect’s headquarters in Brooklyn, New York, large tables are filled with lasers, lenses, special crystals and other tiny components that researchers use to manipulate light. They are all destined to be neatly packaged into bright magenta boxes and then shipped to other builders of the communication technologies of the future.

Against the backdrop of the stunning New York skyline, Namazi opens a box for me, revealing electronics that, at first glance, don’t appear particularly remarkable. But if you stack several of those boxes together, you get what the company calls a Carina rack – and Carina racks can make remarkable quantum things happen.

In February, Qunnect’s team used these racks to perform “entanglement swapping” across 17.6 kilometres of fibre-optic cables connecting Brooklyn and Manhattan via a commercial data centre.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Entanglement swapping is when the property of quantum entanglement is transferred from one pair of photons to another. When photons are entangled, they are extremely sensitive to any attempts to tamper with them, making it nearly impossible to surreptitiously steal information stored within. With swapping, it then becomes possible to extend this unhackable property into a long-distance quantum internet.

Qunnect reliably swapped quantum entanglement between 5400 pairs of photons every hour as the network ran autonomously for days. Previous best experiments swapped entanglement at half that rate or less.

Before a Carina rack can perform its tricks, entangled photons must be created by a separate device. I look at the heart of this “entanglement source” and see a small glass and metal box filled with a vapour of rubidium atoms that must be hit with laser light to produce the photon pairs. The tiniest details matter here. Namazi tells me how his team managed to increase the number of entangled photons they produced by adjusting the angle at which laser beams enter the box.

Once the pairs are created, a Carina rack then sends them through a network of fibre across New York City, for example to labs at New York University and Columbia University.

Namazi explains how I could go about setting up my own entanglement-sharing system if, say, I wanted to send super-secure messages to a friend. “If you have two of these [Carina] racks, you can have distribution of entanglement within a few hours,” says Namazi.

Qunnect keeps one such rack at a commercial data centre in Manhattan, which is operated by telecoms firm QTD Systems. When I ask QTD’s Peter Feldman about it, he echoes Namazi’s sentiment. “I don’t have to know anything about quantum physics,” he says. Devices that keep entangled photons running through Qunnect’s network can be controlled remotely and the process can run autonomously for weeks at a time.

Qunnect’s network uses more than 500 kilometres of fibre to connect with research facilities and networks on Long Island, New York state Qunnect

Attempts to build an unhackable quantum internet aren’t unique to New York City. Several other metropolitan quantum networks exist across the world, including in Hefei, China, and Chicago, Illinois. In each case, there is still work to do to reach the full potential, including fixing the problem that photons often get lost over long distances.

But Namazi says having access to quantum entanglement can already be useful. Entangled photons can be mixed into streams of classical information-carrying light and act as a quantum tripwire of sorts, revealing any nefarious attempts to intercept it.

Another near-term use may be for confirming the identity of the person you are exchanging sensitive information with based on their location, says Alexander Gaeta at Columbia University, who collaborates with Qunnect. This is again possible thanks to the quantum nature of the entangled photons. There are dozens of financial institutions in just one neighborhood of New York City that could benefit from such capabilities, says Javad Shabani at New York University. “Once you have the infrastructure, the end users will come, and they are probably across the street.”

Though the quantum internet is still far from mainstream, during a car ride from Qunnect’s headquarters to QTD’s data centre, I am struck by how much is already up and running. As I cross one of New York’s bridges, I think about how many entangled photons have also done the same. Busy quantum New Yorkers with places to be.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough by Qunnect in building practical quantum entanglement devices enabling unhackable communication over metropolitan fiber networks. This development has broad implications for secure communications, benefiting society at large, especially in financial and data security sectors. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights measurable progress in quantum internet infrastructure.
articles.personality_title: Qunnect builds working quantum network under New York City
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum entanglement is a special connection between particles of light, or photons, that makes them very secure for sending information. For many years, scientists have tried to use this to create a secure internet that cannot be hacked.

**What happened** – Qunnect, a company in Brooklyn, New York, has spent nearly ten years developing devices that share entangled photons through fiber-optic cables. In February, they successfully sent entangled photons across 17.6 kilometers of cables between Brooklyn and Manhattan using their Carina racks, special boxes that manage the quantum signals. They managed to swap entanglement between 5400 pairs of photons every hour, which is much faster than before.

**Impact** – This achievement shows that it is possible to build a real quantum network in a big city. The technology can make communication extremely secure, protecting messages from hackers. Banks and other businesses in New York could use this to keep their information safe. It also proves that quantum internet technology is moving from labs into everyday use.

**What's next step** – Qunnect plans to expand their network, connecting more places in New York and beyond with over 500 kilometers of fiber. They will work on reducing photon loss over long distances and improving the system so it can run smoothly for longer times. More users like universities and financial companies may start using it soon.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Qunnect’s quantum network under New York City is a major step toward secure, unhackable communication using entangled photons sent through city fiber cables.
articles.personality_title_fr: Qunnect construit un réseau quantique opérationnel sous New York
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'intrication quantique est un lien spécial entre des particules de lumière, appelées photons, qui rend la transmission d'informations très sécurisée. Depuis des années, les scientifiques essaient d'utiliser cette propriété pour créer un internet sécurisé et inviolable.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Qunnect, une entreprise de Brooklyn, New York, a passé près de dix ans à développer des appareils qui partagent des photons intriqués via des câbles en fibre optique. En février, ils ont réussi à envoyer des photons intriqués sur 17,6 kilomètres de câbles entre Brooklyn et Manhattan grâce à leurs racks Carina, des boîtiers spéciaux qui gèrent les signaux quantiques. Ils ont échangé l'intrication entre 5400 paires de photons par heure, un rythme bien plus rapide qu'avant.

**Impact** – Cette réussite montre qu'il est possible de construire un véritable réseau quantique dans une grande ville. Cette technologie peut rendre la communication extrêmement sûre, protégeant les messages des pirates. Les banques et autres entreprises de New York pourraient l'utiliser pour sécuriser leurs informations. Cela prouve aussi que la technologie de l'internet quantique sort des laboratoires pour un usage quotidien.

**Prochaine étape** – Qunnect prévoit d'étendre son réseau en connectant plus de lieux à New York et ailleurs avec plus de 500 kilomètres de fibre. Ils travailleront à réduire la perte de photons sur de longues distances et à améliorer le système pour qu'il fonctionne plus longtemps sans interruption. Davantage d'utilisateurs comme les universités et les entreprises financières pourraient bientôt l'adopter.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le réseau quantique de Qunnect sous New York est un grand pas vers une communication sécurisée et inviolable grâce à des photons intriqués transmis par la fibre optique de la ville.
articles.personality_title_es: Qunnect crea una red cuántica funcional bajo Nueva York
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El entrelazamiento cuántico es una conexión especial entre partículas de luz, llamadas fotones, que hace que enviar información sea muy seguro. Durante años, los científicos han tratado de usar esto para crear un internet seguro que no se pueda hackear.

**Qué pasó** – Qunnect, una empresa en Brooklyn, Nueva York, pasó casi diez años desarrollando dispositivos que comparten fotones entrelazados a través de cables de fibra óptica. En febrero, lograron enviar fotones entrelazados a lo largo de 17.6 kilómetros de cables entre Brooklyn y Manhattan usando sus racks Carina, cajas especiales que manejan las señales cuánticas. Intercambiaron entrelazamiento entre 5400 pares de fotones cada hora, mucho más rápido que antes.

**Impacto** – Este logro muestra que es posible construir una red cuántica real en una gran ciudad. La tecnología puede hacer la comunicación extremadamente segura, protegiendo los mensajes de los hackers. Los bancos y otras empresas en Nueva York podrían usarla para mantener su información segura. También demuestra que la tecnología del internet cuántico está pasando de los laboratorios al uso cotidiano.

**Próximo paso** – Qunnect planea expandir su red, conectando más lugares en Nueva York y más allá con más de 500 kilómetros de fibra. Trabajarán para reducir la pérdida de fotones a largas distancias y para mejorar el sistema para que funcione sin problemas por más tiempo. Más usuarios como universidades y compañías financieras podrían comenzar a usarla pronto.

**Resumen en una frase** – La red cuántica de Qunnect bajo Nueva York es un paso importante hacia una comunicación segura e inviolable usando fotones entrelazados enviados por cables de fibra de la ciudad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of intertwined glowing threads of light weaving through a stylized map of New York City’s underground fiber network, connecting abstract magenta boxes symbolizing quantum devices, with subtle beams of laser light illuminating a delicate glass and metal cube representing the entanglement source—all set against a soft, simplified skyline silhouette in natural, muted colors.
